meta.title: Night Patrol Alarm
meta.description: Build an alarm system that activates three red LEDs when a button is pressed.
meta.learning_goal: Using a button to trigger multiple LED outputs
meta.code_hint: Read button state and turn all LEDs on when pressed

meta.title: Night Patrol Alarm
meta.description: Build an alarm system that activates three red LEDs when a button is pressed.
meta.learning_goal: Using a button to trigger multiple LED outputs
meta.code_hint: Read button state and turn all LEDs on when pressed

walkthrough.instruction: Place the button onto the breadboard across the center gap. Top legs in row 12, columns E and F. Bottom legs in row 14, columns E and F.
walkthrough.tip: The button bridges the two halves of the breadboard.

walkthrough.instruction: Put the red LED on the breadboard with the long leg (anode) in row 20, column E and the short leg (cathode) in row 19, column E.
walkthrough.tip: The long leg is positive — it's called the anode!

walkthrough.instruction: Put the 220Ω resistor on the breadboard with one leg in row 19, column D and the other leg in row 15, column D.
walkthrough.tip: The resistor limits current so the component doesn't burn out.

walkthrough.instruction: Put the red LED on the breadboard with the long leg (anode) in row 26, column E and the short leg (cathode) in row 25, column E.
walkthrough.tip: The long leg is positive — it's called the anode!

walkthrough.instruction: Put the 220Ω resistor on the breadboard with one leg in row 25, column D and the other leg in row 21, column D.
walkthrough.tip: The resistor limits current so the component doesn't burn out.

walkthrough.instruction: Put the red LED on the breadboard with the long leg (anode) in row 20, column F and the short leg (cathode) in row 19, column F.
walkthrough.tip: The long leg is positive — it's called the anode!

walkthrough.instruction: Put the 220Ω resistor on the breadboard with one leg in row 19, column G and the other leg in row 15, column G.
walkthrough.tip: The resistor limits current so the component doesn't burn out.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a green wire from Arduino pin D4 to the breadboard at row 12, column A.
walkthrough.tip: This wire carries the signal between Arduino and your component.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a black wire from the breadboard at row 14, column G to the negative (−) rail at row 14.
walkthrough.tip: Black wires carry ground — the return path for electricity.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a green wire from Arduino pin D12 to the breadboard at row 20, column A.
walkthrough.tip: This wire carries the signal between Arduino and your component.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a black wire from the breadboard at row 15, column E to the negative (−) rail at row 15.
walkthrough.tip: Black wires carry ground — the return path for electricity.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a green wire from Arduino pin D8 to the breadboard at row 26, column A.
walkthrough.tip: This wire carries the signal between Arduino and your component.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a black wire from the breadboard at row 21, column E to the negative (−) rail at row 21.
walkthrough.tip: Black wires carry ground — the return path for electricity.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a green wire from Arduino pin D6 to the breadboard at row 20, column J.
walkthrough.tip: This wire carries the signal between Arduino and your component.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a black wire from the breadboard at row 15, column F to the negative (−) rail at row 16.
walkthrough.tip: Black wires carry ground — the return path for electricity.

walkthrough.instruction: Connect a black wire from the Arduino's GND pin to the negative (−) rail at row 30.
walkthrough.tip: Black wires carry ground — the return path for electricity.
